meta.name: Cardiogenic Shock — Anterior STEMI with Pulmonary Edema, Cautious Fluids, Pressors, CPAP

patient.name: Henry Pavlik
patient.chiefComplaint: Severe shortness of breath, cold sweat, and crushing chest pressure that began about 4 hours ago and has progressively worsened; he sat through it 'thinking it was indigestion' until his adult son visited and called 911.

history.hpi: 68-year-old retired warehouse foreman developed substernal chest pressure with diaphoresis approximately 4 hours ago while watching a basketball game with a beer. He attributed it to indigestion, took two Tums, and 'rode it out' alone in the recliner. Over the next 3 hours he became progressively more short of breath, started sweating heavily, and felt 'like the room was closing in.' His adult son stopped by to check on him, found him pale, gray, and gasping, and called 911. The patient now describes substernal pressure 'like a brick on my chest' rated 8/10, radiating to his left arm and jaw, with severe shortness of breath that prevents him from finishing a sentence. He has 30+ pack-year smoking history (still a half-pack per day), hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and a prior NSTEMI 6 years ago treated medically (no stent placed at that time, on aspirin and statin since). Denies sildenafil/tadalafil use. Denies recent dental work or fevers.
pastMedical: Coronary artery disease with prior NSTEMI 2020 (medical management — no stent)
Type 2 diabetes mellitus (poorly controlled, last A1c 9.1%)
Active tobacco use — 30+ pack-years, currently half-pack/day
history.lastOralIntake: Beer and pretzels around the time pain started ~4 hours ago; nothing since
history.events: Substernal chest pressure with diaphoresis ~4 hours ago, attributed to indigestion. Progressive shortness of breath over the next 3 hours. Son arrived, found him pale and gray, called 911. No prior similar event of this severity since the 2020 NSTEMI. No syncope, no palpitations, no recent erectile-dysfunction medication use.

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 68-year-old male, complaint of chest pain and shortness of breath at home. Conscious, working hard to breathe per son. ALS response.
scene.location: Living room of a modest two-story house. Patient slumped forward in a leather recliner, unable to lie back. Front door propped open by son; clear stretcher path through the foyer.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Adult son cooperative and has medication list and discharge papers from prior NSTEMI ready.
scene.bystanders: Adult son arrived ~30 minutes ago and called 911 when he saw the patient gray and gasping. Provides history reliably. No CPR performed; patient has been awake and talking but worsening.
visualCues: Heavyset older man in tripod position in a recliner, leaning forward on his knees
Pale, gray-tinged, profusely diaphoretic — sweat dripping off forehead
Speaking in 1–2 word sentences between breaths
Pink-tinged frothy sputum on a tissue beside the chair
Empty beer can and Tums roll on the side table
Cigarette pack on the mantel; no oxygen equipment or inhaler in use

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS finds a 68-year-old man in obvious cardiogenic shock: pale, diaphoretic, cool extremities, sitting forward with frothy pink sputum, and unable to speak more than 1–2 words. Initial monitor shows sinus tachycardia at 118 with ST elevation in V1–V4 — anterior STEMI. BP is 78/52, lungs reveal rales to the mid-fields, and SpO2 is 86% on room air. This is cardiogenic shock with pulmonary edema — the protocol pathway is FUNDAMENTALLY DIFFERENT from straight septic or hypovolemic shock: a SMALL test fluid bolus (250 mL), early vasopressor support, CPAP for the pulmonary edema, and STEMI activation. Aggressive crystalloid here will worsen pulmonary edema. Nitrates are CONTRAINDICATED with this BP. The teaching point is recognition of the pump failure pattern + restraint with fluids + early pressor.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent — speaking but only 1–2 words at a time
clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 32, severe distress, accessory muscle use, intercostal retractions, tripoding, rales to mid-lung fields bilaterally
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Weak rapid radial pulse; cool, mottled extremities; cap refill 5 sec; flat neck veins not visible due to position but JVD present with patient sitting up
otherFindings: 12-lead: ST elevation V1–V4 — anterior STEMI
Sinus tachycardia at 118 with occasional PVCs
SBP 78 — hypotensive (cardiogenic shock)
Pulmonary edema — rales to mid-fields, pink frothy sputum
Cool/mottled extremities, prolonged cap refill — poor perfusion
EtCO2 28 mmHg — consistent with shock state
Cardiogenic shock pathway: SMALL bolus, early pressor, CPAP, STEMI activation
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (gasping, 1–2 words) Can't… breathe. Chest… brick. Help.

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize cardiogenic shock: cool/mottled, hypotension, AMS-trending, pulm edema, ↓EtCO2
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Pertinent Assessment Findings — cardiogenic/hypovolemic/obstructive shock characterized by cool mottled extremities, delayed cap refill, diminished pulses, and hypotension; EtCO2 <25 may signal poor perfusion.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Shock
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Pertinent Assessment Findings: Cardiogenic, hypovolemic, obstructive shock: capillary refill greater than 2 seconds, diminished peripheral pulses, mottled cool extremities. Pulse oximetry and EtCO2 (reading of less than 25 mmHg may be sign of poor perfusion).

expectedActions.shortAction: 12-lead immediately; identify anterior STEMI; activate STEMI alert with EKG transmission
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS/STEMI step 4 — 12-lead within 10 minutes; advance notification to receiving hospital for STEMI; performance measure tracks contact-to-EKG and EKG-to-notification times.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: The 12-lead EKG is the primary diagnostic tool that identifies a STEMI; it is imperative that EMS clinicians routinely acquire a 12-lead EKG within 10 minutes for all patients exhibiting signs and symptoms of ACS. Advance notification should be provided to the receiving hospital for patients identified as having a STEMI.

expectedActions.shortAction: Aspirin 324 mg PO chewed (4× 81 mg) — non-enteric coated
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS/STEMI step 5 — chewable, non-enteric-coated aspirin 162–325 mg.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Administer aspirin; chewable, non-enteric-coated aspirin preferred (162–325 mg).

expectedActions.shortAction: Withhold nitroglycerin — SBP 78 too low; document hold reason
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS/STEMI step 7 — care should always be taken when BP is marginal; with SBP <100 nitrates risk catastrophic hypotension. Pulmonary-edema branch of resp-respiratory-distress also requires SBP-stratified nitro dosing only above thresholds.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual (SL), can repeat q (quaque, every) 3–5 minutes if SBP greater than 100 mmHg. Care should always be taken when giving nitroglycerin when the patient's blood pressure is marginal. If used in this setting, the clinician should weigh the risk and benefit of nitrate administration over the administration of an opiate analgesic and be ready to respond to hypotension with fluid bolus or pressor.

expectedActions.shortAction: IV access ×2 large-bore; cardiac monitor; SpO2 + EtCO2; defib pads on
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Treatment 3–8 — cardiac monitor, SpO2/EtCO2, IV access (or IO if 90 sec/2 attempts).
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Shock
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: Check vital signs. Administer oxygen as appropriate with a target of achieving 94–98% saturation. Cardiac monitor. Pulse oximetry and EtCO2 (reading of less than 25 mmHg may be sign of poor perfusion). Establish IV access. If unable to obtain within two attempts or less than 90 seconds, place an IO needle.

expectedActions.shortAction: Check blood glucose — rule out reversible cause
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Treatment 5 — check blood sugar, correct if <60.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Shock
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Check blood sugar, and correct if less than 60 mg/dL.

expectedActions.shortAction: Prepare CPAP for pulmonary edema; titrate after small fluid + pressor stabilize BP
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Pulmonary Edema — restoration of oxygenation/ventilation with CPAP/BiPAP precedes or runs alongside other therapies; CPAP indicated for severe distress.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Adults with suspected pulmonary edema due to acute heart failure or fluid overload: Restoration of adequate oxygenation and ventilation should precede or be accomplished simultaneously with other medication therapies below. CPAP/BiPAP: See Airway Management Guideline for goals of care and escalation of interventions.

transitions.if: Cautious 250 mL bolus + early pressor + CPAP
transitions.condition: Crew gives a small 250 mL test bolus only, starts norepinephrine drip (or push-dose epi) titrated to MAP >65, and applies CPAP for pulmonary edema; BP and oxygenation improve, work of breathing decreases.

transitions.if: Aggressive 2 L bolus given (no pressor, no CPAP yet)
transitions.condition: Crew defaults to a generic '30 mL/kg' septic-style resuscitation and pressure-infuses 2 L of crystalloid without recognizing cardiogenic shock; pulmonary edema worsens dramatically, SpO2 and BP fall, patient nearing respiratory failure.

phases.name: Cautious bolus + pressor + CPAP
phases.description: Crew gives a small 250 mL NS test bolus while preparing norepinephrine. Norepinephrine drip started at 5 mcg/min (within 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min range — for 90 kg, 0.06 mcg/kg/min ≈ 5 mcg/min) titrated to MAP >65. CPAP applied at 5–10 cm H2O with high-flow O2. Within 5 minutes BP rises to 96/64 and SpO2 to 94%; rales recede slightly with CPAP. Patient remains in pain but mentation improves and he is now speaking in short phrases. STEMI alert active; transport to PCI-capable center underway.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew chose the cardiogenic-shock pathway: cautious 250 mL test bolus, early norepinephrine titrated to MAP >65, and CPAP for pulmonary edema; perfusion and oxygenation improve.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent under CPAP mask, good seal
clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 24, less labored on CPAP, accessory muscle use decreasing, rales receding to bases
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Pulse 110 regular; skin warming; cap refill 3 sec

skin.color: improving — less gray, returning pink at lips

otherFindings: 250 mL NS bolus given without worsening crackles
Norepinephrine drip running at 5 mcg/min, titrated to MAP >65
CPAP 5 cm H2O, then 10 cm H2O with FiO2 100% — tolerating
BP 96/64, MAP ~75, SpO2 94%, EtCO2 32
Mentation improving — speaking in short phrases now
Anterior STEMI alert called; PCI center accepting; transport active
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (short phrases through CPAP) Easier to breathe. Still hurts. Thank you.

expectedActions.shortAction: 250 mL NS test bolus only — STOP if rales worsen (cardiogenic-shock fluid rule)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Pulm Edema SBP <100 — IV fluid bolus 250–500 mL; MATC Shock Patient Safety — if patient deteriorates after fluid or rales develop, withhold further fluid (cardiogenic shock).
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: SBP less than 100 mmHg: IV fluid bolus 250–500 mL. Consider vasopressor: Norepinephrine 0.02–2 mcg/kg/min.

expectedActions.shortAction: Norepinephrine 5 mcg/min IV (90 kg → ~0.06 mcg/kg/min) titrate to MAP >65
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Vasopressors 11a-i — norepinephrine 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min titrated to MAP 65; preferred in cardiogenic and septic shock.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Shock
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Vasopressors (shock unresponsive to IV fluids) titrated to physiologic targets: Cardiogenic, hypovolemic, obstructive shock and distributive shock: Norepinephrine 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/minute. Preference in both neurogenic and infectious (sepsis) causes of distributive shock.

expectedActions.shortAction: Push-dose epi 10 mcg IV ready (1 mL of 10 mcg/mL dilution) if BP collapses while drip runs
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Vasopressors 11a-ii — push-dose epi 10 mcg q3–5 min as alternative/adjunct to drip while titrating up.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Shock
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Epinephrine, 0.05–0.3 mcg/kg/minute. Alternative to a drip, push dose epinephrine may be administered: Prepare 10 mcg/mL by diluting 1 mL of epinephrine 0.1 mg/mL (1:10,000) in 9 mL of normal saline. Administer 0.01 mg/kg (0.1 mL/kg) up to a maximum single dose of 10 mcg (1 mL) q 3–5 minutes titrated to maintain goal MAP.

expectedActions.shortAction: CPAP 5→10 cm H2O with high FiO2; titrate to SpO2 94–98% and reduced WOB
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Pulm Edema — restoration of oxygenation/ventilation with CPAP/BiPAP per Airway Management Guideline; non-invasive ventilation for severe distress.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Non-invasive ventilation (NIV) should be administered for severe respiratory distress via BVM, continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) or bi-level positive airway pressure (BiPAP). Adults with suspected pulmonary edema due to acute heart failure or fluid overload: Restoration of adequate oxygenation and ventilation should precede or be accomplished simultaneously with other medication therapies below. CPAP/BiPAP: See Airway Management Guideline for goals of care and escalation of interventions.

expectedActions.shortAction: STEMI alert; transport directly to PCI-capable hospital; serial 12-leads en route
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS/STEMI step 10–11 + Performance Measures — destination based on system of care; advance notification; serial 12-leads.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Transport and destination decisions should be based on local resources and system of care. Early notification to receiving facility of any changes in patient condition or serial EKGs. Performance of serial EKGs is encouraged for symptomatic patients with EKGs initially non-diagnostic for STEMI.

transitions.if: Stable on pressor + CPAP; transport to cath lab
transitions.condition: Hemodynamics improving on norepinephrine + CPAP; STEMI alert active; patient en route to PCI-capable hospital with continued reassessment of perfusion, oxygenation, and rhythm.

phases.name: Stable on pressor + CPAP; transport to cath lab
phases.description: Patient transported to PCI-capable hospital with norepinephrine drip titrated and CPAP continued. Reassessing perfusion, MAP, oxygenation, and rhythm en route. Watching for VT/VF (anterior MI risk), hypotension breakthrough, or pulmonary edema rebound. Bedside handoff to interventional cardiology team includes door-to-EKG time, time of STEMI activation, all medications and drips, and the patient's anticipatory pain control discussion (opiates withheld so far due to marginal BP).
phases.triggerCondition: Hemodynamics stabilized on norepinephrine + CPAP; transport active to PCI-capable hospital with continuous monitoring.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 22, decreased work of breathing, rales receding
otherFindings: MAP holding 75–80 on norepinephrine 5–8 mcg/min
Defib pads in place; sync mode pre-armed in case of VT
Serial 12-lead unchanged: anterior STEMI
Patient reporting pain has decreased with improved perfusion (still 6/10)
Receiving cardiology updated; cath team mobilized
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: I can talk now. Still got the pain but it's not as bad. Thank you for coming.

expectedActions.shortAction: Continuous monitor for VT/VF; pads stay on; defib mode ready
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS/STEMI Patient Safety #3, #4 — defibrillator pads on high-risk patients; configure VT/VF alert; anterior MI is high-arrhythmia-risk territory.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Observe for signs of clinical deterioration: dysrhythmias, chest pain, shortness of breath, decreased level of consciousness/syncope, or other signs of shock/hypotension. Consider placing defibrillator pads on high-risk patients. Consider configuring monitor/defibrillator to allow automatic VT/VF alert.

expectedActions.shortAction: Reassess vitals + neuro + lung exam q5 min; titrate norepi to MAP >65
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Documentation — full vitals with reassessment q15 min or more frequently as appropriate; norepinephrine titration is continuous.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Shock
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Medications administered. Full vital signs (pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, neurologic status assessment) with reassessment q 15 minutes or more frequently as appropriate.

expectedActions.shortAction: Update receiving hospital with vitals, dose timeline, and ETA; handoff at bedside with strips
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS/STEMI step 11 — early notification of any changes; STEMI patients require advance notification of receiving facility.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Advance notification should be provided to the receiving hospital for patients identified as having a STEMI. Early notification to receiving facility of any changes in patient condition or serial EKGs. All EKGs should be made available to treating personnel at the receiving hospital, whether hand delivered as hard copy or transmitted from the field.

phases.name: Aggressive 2 L bolus given
phases.description: Crew anchored on a generic 'shock = fluids' approach and pressure-infused 2 L of NS without recognizing the cardiogenic pattern (cool/mottled, rales, frothy sputum, anterior STEMI). Within minutes the patient's pulmonary edema worsens dramatically — SpO2 falls into the 70s, rales rise to the apices, frothy pink sputum increases, and BP actually FALLS as the failing left ventricle decompensates further. The MATC Shock protocol explicitly states: 'If the patient condition deteriorates after fluid administration, rales or hepatomegaly develop, then consider cardiogenic shock and withholding further fluid administration.' The crew must STOP fluids immediately, escalate to CPAP/BVM with PEEP, start a vasopressor, and prepare for possible airway intervention if respiratory failure progresses.
phases.triggerCondition: Aggressive 2 L crystalloid resuscitation precipitated worsening pulmonary edema and falling BP — classic cardiogenic-shock fluid trap; protocol requires withholding further fluid and pivoting to pressor + ventilatory support.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Frothy pink sputum at the mouth and nares; suction needed
clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 38, agonal-leaning, accessory muscle exhaustion, rales to the apices, audible gurgling
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Weak rapid pulse; cool, mottled; cap refill 6 sec
otherFindings: 2 L NS pressure-infused — pulmonary edema dramatically worse
SpO2 dropping into the 70s on NRB
BP falling further (now 64/40) as LV decompensates
Rales to the apices; frothy pink sputum increasing
Patient lethargic, can no longer speak in phrases
Imminent respiratory failure — CPAP/BVM with PEEP urgent + start pressor

expectedActions.shortAction: STOP IV fluids; recognize deterioration after bolus = cardiogenic shock
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Patient Safety — if patient deteriorates after fluid or rales/hepatomegaly develop, consider cardiogenic shock and withhold further fluid.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Shock
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Recognition of cardiogenic shock - If the patient condition deteriorates after fluid administration, rales or hepatomegaly develop, then consider cardiogenic shock and withholding further fluid administration.

expectedActions.shortAction: BVM with PEEP valve (or CPAP if tolerated); suction frothy sputum; FiO2 100%
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Pulm Edema — restoration of oxygenation/ventilation precedes other therapies; CPAP/BiPAP/BVM with PEEP for severe distress and impending failure.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Non-invasive ventilation (NIV) should be administered for severe respiratory distress via BVM, continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) or bi-level positive airway pressure (BiPAP). Restoration of adequate oxygenation and ventilation should precede or be accomplished simultaneously with other medication therapies below.

expectedActions.shortAction: Start norepinephrine 5–10 mcg/min (≈0.06–0.11 mcg/kg/min for 90 kg) titrate to MAP >65
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Vasopressors 11a-i — norepinephrine 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min titrated to MAP 65; preferred for cardiogenic shock unresponsive to fluids.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Shock
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Vasopressors (shock unresponsive to IV fluids) titrated to physiologic targets: Cardiogenic, hypovolemic, obstructive shock and distributive shock: Norepinephrine 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/minute.

expectedActions.shortAction: Push-dose epi 10 mcg IV as bridge while drip is prepared
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Vasopressors — push-dose epi as alternative to drip; useful as a bridge for time-sensitive collapse.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Shock
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Alternative to a drip, push dose epinephrine may be administered: Prepare 10 mcg/mL by diluting 1 mL of epinephrine 0.1 mg/mL (1:10,000) in 9 mL of normal saline. Administer 0.01 mg/kg (0.1 mL/kg) up to a maximum single dose of 10 mcg (1 mL) q 3–5 minutes titrated to maintain goal MAP.

expectedActions.shortAction: STEMI alert remains active; consider divert if respiratory failure progresses
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS/STEMI step 10–11 — destination based on system of care; advance notification with any change in condition.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Chest Pain/Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)/ST-segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Transport and destination decisions should be based on local resources and system of care. Early notification to receiving facility of any changes in patient condition or serial EKGs.
